conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do! I have three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, Blake, and Charlie, I need to ask you some questions about each child.

Let's start with their ages. How old are Alex, Blake, and Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 7 years old, Blake is 20, and Charlie is also 7 - Alex and Charlie are twins.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Now I need to understand more about Blake, who is 20 years old, as there are specific rules for children over 16.

Is Blake currently in approved education or training (such as full-time non-advanced education or approved training)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in any education or training at the moment. He's actually working full-time now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that about Blake. Now I need to ask about your living arrangements with the children.

Do Alex, Blake, and Charlie all live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they all live with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. Now I need to understand your residency status. Are you currently living in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have one more important question to complete my assessment.

Are you responsible for Alex, Blake, and Charlie? This means you're the one who looks after them and pays towards their upbringing (such as for food, clothes, and other needs).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm responsible for all of them. I look after them and pay for their food, clothes, and everything they need.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, here's my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility:

**You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for 2 out of your 3 children:**

✅ **Alex (age 7)** - **Eligible**
You can claim Child Benefit for Alex because they are under 16, live with you, you are responsible for them, and you live in the UK.

❌ **Blake (age 20)** - **Not Eligible**
Unfortunately, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake. While children under 16 automatically qualify, those aged 16 or over only qualify if they are in approved education or training. Since Blake is 20 and working full-time (not in education or training), he does not meet the eligibility criteria.

✅ **Charlie (age 7)** - **Eligible**
You can claim Child Benefit for Charlie because they are under 16, live with you, you are responsible for them, and you live in the UK.

**Summary:** You can claim Child Benefit for your twins Alex and Charlie. If you haven't already, you should apply for Child Benefit to receive payments for these two children.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 7 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 20 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 7 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 20, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 7 years old, which is under 16. Alex lives with you, you are responsible for Alex, and you live in the UK. According to the eligibility rules, you can get Child Benefit for a child under 16 if you're responsible for them and they live with you or you're paying at least the same as Child Benefit or the equivalent towards looking after them. Alex meets all these criteria, so you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 20 years old and is not in approved education or training - he is working full-time. According to the eligibility rules, you can only claim Child Benefit for a young person aged 16 or over if they are in approved education or training. Since Blake is over 16 and not in approved education or training, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

Charlie is 7 years old, which is under 16. Charlie lives with you, you are responsible for Charlie, and you live in the UK. According to the eligibility rules, you can get Child Benefit for a child under 16 if you're responsible for them and they live with you or you're paying at least the same as Child Benefit or the equivalent towards looking after them. Charlie meets all these criteria, so you are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.

response.overall_reasoning: You have three children: Alex (7), Blake (20), and Charlie (7). You live in the UK, all children live with you, and you are responsible for them. You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex and Charlie as they are both under 16. However, you are not eligible for Blake as he is 20 years old and not in approved education or training. You can claim Child Benefit for 2 out of your 3 children.
